trackingsummarydata.subject: This week in A&S: Home, human historians and why women won
trackingsummarydata.title: FSSG - 09/22/25

urls.page_title: Holocaust testimony is AI litmus test, and it fails
urls.page_summary: A Cornell historian argues that human historians are vital to capture the emotional and moral complexity behind world events.
urls.page_title: ‘Sacred Ground’ exhibit receives Historic Ithaca preservation award
urls.page_summary: Open now through Dec. 31, the exhibit highlights findings from a four-year archaeological excavation of Ithaca’s St. James A.M.E. Zion Church conducted by Cornell faculty, students and Ithaca school children from 2021–2024.
urls.page_title: Nobel-winning economist to speak on ‘why women won’
urls.page_summary: Claudia Goldin ’67, winner of the 2023 Nobel Prize in Economics, will return to campus to give the 2025 Staller Lecture on Sept. 25.
urls.page_title: Emr wins the World Laureate Association Prize
urls.page_summary: The award is for breakthrough discoveries in the cellular mechanisms of receptor membrane protein transport and degradation.
urls.page_title: Sense of place trumps tax breaks in choosing where to live | Cornell Chronicle
urls.page_summary: There’s no place like home — and even when state-by-state income tax disparities make it profitable to move, high-wage earners seem to agree, according to new Cornell-led research.
urls.page_title: The Cornell Astronomical Society & Fuertes Observatory
urls.page_summary: The Cornell Astronomical Society hosts public observing nights at the Fuertes Observatory every Friday evening from 8pm to midnight.
Awards and honors received by faculty, postdocs and graduate students in the College of Arts & Sciences.
urls.page_title: PMA at Sundance 2026: Information Sessions | Performing and Media Arts
